coredata.dc:description: Incomplete reduction of oxygen produces reactive oxygen species (ROS) or intermediates. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide (O2 •−), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and hypochlorite (ClO-) are examples of ROS. Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) belongs to reactive nitrogen species. Recent studies are evidenced that they play crucial roles in cell signaling and tissue homeostasis. Further, ROS are recognized as oxidizing chemical species for various biochemical pathways (oxidative stress and damage of organisms) and as scavengers for various environmental applications. Herein, the recent progress of nanomaterials as optical sensors for colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of reactive oxygen species is discussed. This review also provides the analytical features of nanomaterials-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for sensing of ROS in real samples (biological and environmental samples). Additionally, the future prospects of nanomaterials-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for the detection of ROS are discussed.

head.ce:title: Recent progress of nanomaterials for colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of reactive oxygen species in biological and environmental samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Incomplete reduction of oxygen produces reactive oxygen species (ROS) or intermediates. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide (O2 •−), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and hypochlorite (ClO-) are examples of ROS. Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) belongs to reactive nitrogen species. Recent studies are evidenced that they play crucial roles in cell signaling and tissue homeostasis. Further, ROS are recognized as oxidizing chemical species for various biochemical pathways (oxidative stress and damage of organisms) and as scavengers for various environmental applications. Herein, the recent progress of nanomaterials as optical sensors for colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of reactive oxygen species is discussed. This review also provides the analytical features of nanomaterials-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for sensing of ROS in real samples (biological and environmental samples). Additionally, the future prospects of nanomaterials-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for the detection of ROS are discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) consist of at least one oxygen atom those have unpaired electrons (superoxide and hydroxyl, peroxyl ions and other species) [1]. Usually, ROS (radical species: hydroxy radical (•OH), superoxide anion (O2 •–), and non-radical species: hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and singlet oxygen (1O2)) can be formed by the incomplete reduction of oxygen, exhibiting key roles in a number of physiological pathways in the organisms [2]. Peroxynitrite (ONOO-) is generated via diffusion-controlled reaction of NO with O2 •−. It was also observed that the generation of ROS with higher yields results oxidative stress in the living cells, thereby creating multiple inflammatory situations in the cells [3]. Further, ROS have shown to be potential reactive chemical species for the degradation of various chemicals in environmental samples [4]. They have been used for the degradation of organic compounds and microbial species [5,6]. In view of this, •OH, O2 •–, ONOO-, ClO- and H2O2 [7–10] have been detected by various analytical techniques. ROS are recognized as signaling chemical species and produced during the metabolic process, involving in several biochemical pathways in cells. Importantly, •OH, ClO-, and ONOO- are highly ROS because of their strong oxidant characteristics, displaying high ability to direct attack nucleic acids, proteins, and lipids in live cells, which causes potentially severe damage [11]. These reports revealed that ROS play key roles in living cells and environmental remediation. Thus, the identification of ROS in biological and environmental samples is very important for understanding biopathways in cells and degradation of toxic chemical compounds for environmental remediation.

The detection and quantification of ROS in biological and environmental samples are very essential and prerequisite in multidisciplinary research. The monitoring of ROS in complex samples is a challenging task due to their high reactivity and short lifetimes (O2 •− - 5 - several hundred thousand μs, 1O2 - 2 μs and •OH - 200 μs) [4]. The detection of ROS and radioactive elements was performed by conventional analytical approaches such as high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical sensors, fluorescence, UV-Visible, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometric techniques [4–6,10,12]. Although these analytical techniques exhibited high potentiality to identify ROS, unfortunately some of the analytical strategies are expensive, poor sensitivity, time-consuming and required tedious sample preparations, which may lead to impact on the analytical figures (selectivity, sensitivity, accuracy and precision) of the methods. Thus, there is an urgent demand to establish effective portable analytical methods that permit the detection of ROS in real samples with several analytical features including rapidity, sensitivity and selectivity at minimal volume of samples, respectively. Recent years, colorimetric and fluorescence analytical methods have found to be promising portable analytical methods for the detection of trace level analytes [13,14]. In these assays, the target analytes can be detected based on the remarkable change in color (absorption) or fluorescence spectral characteristics, thereby allowing to visualize color changes with naked eye, followed by their quantification via UV–visible and fluorescence spectrometry [15–18]. The conventional UV–visible and fluorescence methods have some limitations for the detection of ROS in real samples such as low sensitivity due to the extinction coefficients of chromophores, color shading of organic chromophores, tedious synthetic protocols for the preparation of organic fluorophores, leading to make them as incapable for sensing of ROS with higher sensitivity.

Over the past few decades, nanomaterials-based analytical (colorimetric and fluorescence) methods have been conceptualized for the detection of ROS in biological and environmental samples due to their unique optical and physical properties [19–25]. The integration of nanostructure materials with optical spectroscopic methods (absorption and fluorescence) are providing new insights to extend their remarkable analytical applications such as colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for the detection of ROS [20–28]. In addition, the nanoparticles-based fluorescent/colorometric detection has opened up new avenues for the development of detecting probes, sensors, and theranostic products [2]. The above reviews have been completely summarized the development of colorimetric and fluorescence methods for the identification of ROS using organic molecules as probes, none of the review has focused on the coupling of nanomaterials with absorption and fluorescence spectrometry for the identification of ROS. In this review, we intend to present an overview on the recent progress of nanomaterials as colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for the detection of ROS in environmental and biological samples. First section describes the development of colorimetric methods for sensing of ROS using various nanomaterials (metal nanoparticles (NPs), metal nanoclusters (NCs), metal oxides, carbon nitrides and hybrid nanostructures) as colorimetric probes. Second section summarizes the fluorescence detection of ROS using various nanostructure materials such as quantum dots (QDs), metal NCs, carbon dots (CDs) and hybrid nanocomposites as fluorescent nanosensors. This review discussed the nanomaterials-based optical (absorbance and fluorescence) methods for the detection of ROS (H2O2, ClO-, •O2 -, •OH, ClO- and ONOO-) in various matrices. Finally, perspectives of nanomaterials-based optical sensors for ROS detection in environmental and biological samples are also discussed.

It is well known that ROS act as a messenger for various metabolic pathways in cell signal transduction and cycling [29]. Noticeably, excess levels of reactive oxygen species cause irreparable cells damage via oxidative of biopolymers, nucleic acid, lipids and carbohydrates, which results cell death via necrosis or apoptosis [30]. As a result, knowing the precise locations and quantities of ROS is crucial for determining their biological effects. However, because of the instability, short life, and mutual interaction of most ROS, achieving all the requirements listed above is difficult [25]. Notwithstanding the difficulties of detecting ROS, many types of analytical approaches including X-ray fluorescence microscopy, chemiluminescent probes, ESR spectroscopy, colorimetry, chromatography and molecular biology-based fluorescence methods [6] have been developed and commonly used as conventional methods for the identification and quantification of ROS. However, most of those conventional techniques cannot detect in-situ generated ROS and low concentrations, which need additional sensitivity. Recently, fluorescence and electrochemical analytical approaches have proven to be inexpensive and rapid techniques for •O2 - detection among the various techniques available [31–33]. Fluorescent probes provide profiles of radical formation under stimulus. These probes are oxidized via a one-electron free radical mechanism, resulting to form fluorescence derivatives. For instance, dihydroethidium (DHE), dichlorodihydro fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and Amplex Red are widely used for quantitative fluorescence detection of ROS in various biological samples [6]. Although these probes have been identified ROS in tissues and cells, however, the analytical figures of those methods are still not satisfactory for real sample analysis [1,4,33]. Furthermore, the reaction stoichiometry between •O2 - and DHE not consistency and specific [34,35]. Thus, the introduction of nanomaterials as optical probes (colorimetric and fluorescent) for the detection of trace level ROS has created new opportunities for monitoring of ROS in complex samples because nanomaterial scaffolds enhance optical properties, permit tunable interactions with cells and media, which enables ratiometric sensing that is resistant to environmental drift. Thus, first section overviews the use of different types of nanomaterials as colorimetric sensor for the detection of ROS in cells and environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials for colorimetric sensing of ROS
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the development of nanomaterials-based colorimetric assay has received much attention in the field of bioanalytical and analytical sciences for the rapid and sensitive detection of wide variety molecules from complex samples [15,16]. Nanomaterials-based colorimetric sensors exhibited several remarkable merits such as rapidity, simplicity, sensitivity and selectivity, favoring to improve sensitivity and selectivity of absorption spectrometry, which can be a powerful analytical tool for the detection of ROS. Various nanomaterials (metal NPs, carbon nitride, metal oxides and hybrid nanostructures) have been used as colorimetric sensors for the identification of ROS, offering a superior dimensionality to ROS sensing with ultra-sensitivity and color discrimination among ROS species. Scheme 1 illustrates that the colorimetric sensing applications of nanomaterials for the detection of ROS.

Hydrogen peroxide is one of the most attractive ROS. It is also the most common ROS in industrial, food, clinical, biological and medical settings [36]·H2O2 has longer lifetime than other ROS. Fluorescence microscopic studies revealed that H2O2 is easily entered into across cell membranes, playing key roles in various biochemical pathways in cells [37], which signifies those elevated levels of H2O2 may cause several disorders such as diabetes, cancer, Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases [38,39]. As a result, H2O2 could be used as a biomarker to detect the onset of a variety of oxidative-stress-related diseases [4]. Importantly, H2O2 will be generated in cells via the stimulation of nonphagocytic cells. Under normal conditions, trace level of H2O2 can effectively inhibit the growth of microorganisms to maintain the cell functions [2]. However, higher concentration of H2O2 (10–50 μM) will be produced by cells, promoting the growth of tumor cells and epithelial cells, which signifies that the concentration of H2O2 could be used as a biomarker for the differentiation of cells functions [5]·H2O2 is another of the most widely studied ROS, and its detection has primarily relied on analytical techniques including spectrophotometry, fluorescence, chemiluminescence (CL), and electrochemistry [40].

Peroxidase activity has been discovered in ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets [S1, “S” defines the reference in Supporting Information]. For this study, author used 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) for the catalysation of oxidation. The peroxidase capability of Fe–g–C3N4 was used to develop a colorimetric method for H2O2 measurement. In this reaction, Fe2+ ions convert H2O2 to •OH, which initiates the catalytic oxidation of TMB. As a result, noticeable spectral changes were observed in the solution of TMB, TMB/H2O2 and TMB/g-C3N4 in the presence of H2O2, a good linearity was noticed with increasing concentration of H2O2, which allows to develop a simple colorimetric approach for the detection of H2O2.

The catalytic activity of g-C3N4 was investigated by the oxidation of TMB using H2O2 to generate the characteristic blue color, which was highly dependent on catalyst concentration, H2O2 concentration, pH, and temperature [S2]. The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor has greatly improved by Cu NPs modified g-C3N4 nanosheet [S3]. The catalytic activity of g-C3N4 was increased by the doping of Cu NPs into g-C3N4 nanosheets since Cu NPs accelerates the electron transfer rate from g-C3N4 to H2O2. This probe provides a good liner range between the intensity and H2O2 (1 × 10−7 – 2 × 10−6 M) with a LOD of 0.032 × 10−6 M. The thermal condensation technique was used to load MnSe NPs on the surface of polymeric g-C3N4 as a co-catalyst by calcinating a combination of pure MnSe, dicyandiamide, and cyanuric acid [S4]. The as-synthesised MnSe-g-C3N4 nanosheets show inherent peroxidase-like activity. The hydrogen bond aided soft template technique was described for the preparation of Zn-g-C3N4 nanosheets [41]. In this method, Zn was successfully doped into mesoporous g-C3N4 using polyethylene glycol-1500 (PEG-1500) as a soft template ( Fig. 1). A fast and single-pot chemical reduction technique was used to examine the effect of capping agents such as polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), PEG, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) on silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and found that PVA-Ag NPs exhibited a greater stability and sensing ability towards H2O2 sensing via localised surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) phenomenon [S5].

A green chemistry approach was described for the preparation of silver chloride from brown algae (Sargassum boveanum) and used for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 in herbal Iranian oxidant Padina [S6]. The as-prepared AgCl-NPs showed maximum absorbance at 417 nm. The SPR peak intensity of AgCl NPs at 417 nm was linear with H2O2 concentration (1–120 μM), with a LOD of 8.64 nM. This method was successfully applied to estimate hydrogen peroxide concentration in herbal Iranian oxidant Padina sample·H2O2 concentration was estimated by forming triangular silver nanoplates [S7]. This method showed higher selectivity, a broad linear range (50 × 10−9 to 1 × 10−6 M) and a low LOD (1.36 × 10−9 M). Small sheets of poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) and AgNO3 were used to produce PMMA-Ag NPs as colorimetric sensors for sensing of H2O2 [S8]. Importantly, PMMA-Ag NPs acted as a selective sensor for the detection of H2O2 even in the presence of chemical species (EtOH, NH3 and acetone).

A hybrid nanomaterial was produced by the combination of Ag NPs and cellulose nanowhiskers (CNW) (CNW-Ag NPs) for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 [42]. The developed sensing platform exhibited several merits such as inexpensive, simplicity and repeatability for sensing of H2O2. It was noticed that the absorbance of CNW-Ag NPs at 410 nm was progressively disappeared with increasing amount of H2O2 (0.01 - 600 μM), favoring to visualize color changes from yellow to colorless ( Fig. 2). The sensing mechanism was based on the oxidation of CNW-Ag NPs to Ag2O by the addition of H2O2 where H2O2 was also decomposed into H2O and oxygen. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images of CNW-Ag NPs revealed that the size of CNW-Ag NPs was decreased with the addition of H2O2, signifying the corrosion of CNW-Ag NPs by H2O2. Practical application of the developed sensor was examined for sensing of H2O2 in real samples (water and milk samples), showing good recoveries in the range of 85–98%, which indicates that this method could be used for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 in real samples. A simple and selective colorimetric sensor was developed for the detection of H2O2 by using Ag NPs as a nanosensor derived from Kiwifruit extract [S9]. The formation of Ag NPs with good spectral and morphological characteristics was highly influenced by silver nitrate concentration as well as the amount of Kiwifruit extract. The intensity of SPR band of Ag NPs at 425 nm was progressively decreased with H2O2 concentration (5.0 × 10−5 – 5.0 × 10−3 M) and successfully utilized as a simple visual reader for sensing of H2O2 in sterilant solutions. Citrate-capped Ag NPs were reported as a colorimetric probe for the identification of H2O2 [S10]. Authors investigated on the influence of Ag NPs morphologies (spherical, cubic and triangular) for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 where oxidative etching highly depends on the morphology of Ag NPs. Triangular Ag NPs showed superior sensitivity toward H2O2, whereas cubic Ag NPs showed very poor sensitivity toward H2O2, which confirms that the shape of Ag NPs is strongly dependent on the sensitivity of Ag NPs-based H2O2 sensor. They confirmed that the oxidative etching was not largely occurred in polyhedral NPs. In addition, the tips/edges of NPs showed higher oxidative etching ability, leading to a larger change in the shape of NPs due to the inherent defects in the formed nanostructures. As a result, the triangular Ag NPs-based colorimetric sensor was effectively applied to detect H2O2 in tap water samples, exhibiting good recoveries from 96% to 100.2%. Huang’s group decorated Ag NPs with tetraphenylethene-silica nanoparticles (TPE-SiO2 @Ag NPs) for the development of a novel dual sensor (colorimetric and fluorometric) for the detection of H2O2 in human serum and cells [S11]. In this work, TPE-SiO2 NPs were prepared and exhibited a strong emission peak at 475 nm due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE), however it was quenched by Ag NPs. Interestingly, H2O2 was effectively detected via colorimetric and fluorometric methods where Ag NPs can oxidize to silver ion by H2O2, which favors to restore emission peak at 475 nm. As a result, H2O2 was detected even at 2.1 and 0.28 μM by colorimetry and fluorescence spectrometry, respectively. The developed dual probe exhibited another remarkable advantage for imaging of H2O2 in HeLa cells.

Yang’s group introduced benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BDBA) as a driving agent for the aggregation of citrate-Au NPs via a strong interaction between boronic acid of BDBA and α-hydroxycarboxylate of citrate [S12]. Authors noticed that the oxidation of BDBA by H2O2, leading to convert boronic acid to phenolic groups. The oxidized BDBA was incapable for triggering the aggregation of AuNPs. However, a sandwich immunoassay was developed for the detection of human-prostate-specific antigen and rabbit immunoglobulin G (IgG) where hydrogen peroxide was used for the conversion of BDBA, which triggers the aggregation of Au NPs. In another report, Au NPs were modified with nucleic acid (deoxyribonucleic acid, DNA) for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 with a LOD of 1 µM [S13]. This method requires 15 min and used Fenton reaction for the production of hydroxyl radical from H2O2, resulting to break DNA molecules on the surface of Au NPs, which yields the aggregation of Au NPs. Noticeably, the SPR band is progressively shifted from 530 to 581 nm with increasing amounts of H2O2 (0–50 µM). They also observed that the LOD of the probe did not improve with increased detection time. Mvango and Mashazi synthesized CuO-Au nanoalloy and investigated their peroxidase-like activity [S14]. It was noticed that CuO-Au nanoalloy acted as nanoenzyme to inhibit peroxidase-like properties by producing ROS. The as-synthesized CuO-Au nanoalloy acted as a probe for colorimetric detection of both hydrogen peroxide and glucose. Another approach described the use of N-doped TiO2 NPs stabilized in ionic liquids (ILs) for sensing of H2O2 [S15]. In the presence of H2O2, N-doped TiO2 NPs exhibited high potentiality for the catalytic oxidation of TMB, yielding a blue color solution. This probe exhibited a good linear response between λmax at 652 nm and concentration of H2O2 (10–300 μM), offering an impressive LOD of 2.5 μM for H2O2.

Another colorimetric approach was developed for sensing of H2O2 by modifying Ni NPs with two different kinds of ILs (imidazolium and pyridinium combination with acetate anion) [S16]. Pyridinium-Ni NPs acted as a promising colorimetric sensor for the detection of H2O2, thereby exhibiting peroxidase-like activity for the catalytic oxidation of TMB in H2O2 environment. The highest oxidation of TMB was noticed at pH 7.4 and at 25 °C, exhibiting impressive LOD of 120 μM within a linear range of H2O2 (400–4000 μM). To improve the selectivity and sensitivity of metal oxide NPs, 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-carboxyl pheyl)-porphyrin (H2TCPP) was successfully functionalized on NiO NPs for generation of peroxidase mimetics, which has ability to catalyze peroxidase compound TMB in H2O2, which yields blue color [S17]. The as-fabricated H2TCPP-NiO NPs showed a lower LOD of 8.0 × 10−6 M, which may offer to establish a facile analytical approach for the detection of hydroxyl radical in biological and environmental samples.

To examine the sensing applications of nanosheets (NSs), NiFe-layered double hydroxide NSs (NiFe-LDHNSs) were synthesized by exfoliating bulk LDH in L-asparagine aqueous medium and produced NiFe-LDHNSs have lateral size of 200–300 nm and thickness < 2 nm with the broken edges [S18]. The colorimetry assay of H2O2 was carried out by the oxidation of TMB into ox-TMB by NiFe-LDHNSs, which has ability to recognize H2O2 even at 4.4 × 10−6 M. A hydrothermal method was used for the preparation of NiS nanostructures with hollow and porous nature using Ni–Co Prussian blue analogue as a precursor [S19]. The addition of Ni-Co nanocubes into H2O2-TBA solution was efficiently induced the color and absorbance (652 nm), and the absorbance at 652 nm is progressively increased with increasing H2O2 concentration. This approach exhibited a good linearity between λmax at 652 nm and H2O2 (4–40 μM), which offers to exhibit lower LOD of 1.72 μM. Authors noticed that enzyme action follows ping-pong mechanism and Michaelis–Menten kinetics for the catalytic oxidation. An ultrasonic-assisted technique was used for the modification of CeO2 nanorods (NRs) with N, N′-dicarboxy methyl perylene diimide (CeO2 NRs-PDI) [S20]. Electron transfer mechanism was established for the detection of H2O2 since the rate of hydroxyl radical (•OH) formation is proportional to the decomposition rate of H2O2. To confirm this, the catalytic reaction mechanism of CeO2-PDI NRs was investigated by using terephthalic acid as a fluorescent molecule. The fluorescence intensity was progressively decreased with increasing concentration of CeO2-PDI nanostructures, which reveals that the •OH species are not generated in the reaction. However, coating of TBA onto the surface of CeO2-PDI nanostructures provides electron rich surface, favoring to accelerate electron transfer between CeO2-PDI and H2O2, which leads to develop catalytic reaction of TMB by H2O2. As a result, a linear calibration graph was generated between λmax at 652 nm and H2O2 (10–500 μM) with a LOD of 2.23 μM. Peroxidase-like activity of ZnS NPs was greatly improved by coating ZnS NPs on to the surface of montmorillonite (ZnS-MMT) [S21]. The oxidation reaction of TMB was rapidly catalyzed in the presence of H2O2, yielding a blue color solution within 30 s. Authors confirmed that ZnS-MMT nanocomposite exhibited superior catalytic activity than that of bare ZnS and MMT. The catalytic behavior of ZnS-MMT follows Michaelis–Menten kinetic model. This method has proven to be an inexpensive and promising platform for sensing of H2O2 in biotechnology and for enzymatic mimics.

Recently, colorimetric H2O2 assay was performed by using Co3(PO4)20.8 H2O as a nanozyme [S22]. Authors used Michaelis-Menten kinetic model to confirm peroxidase-like activity of Co3(PO4)20.8 H2O. It was noticed that a good linearity between λmax at 652 nm and H2O2 (15.0 – 100 × 10−6 M), offering LOD of 4.385 × 10−6 M), which allows to apply it as promising colorimetric tool for the detection of H2O2 in milk samples. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was used as a template for the synthesis of BSA-Cu NCs and used as a probe for sensing of both H2O2 and glucose [S23]. The as-prepared BSA-Cu NCs showed better biocatalytic activity as compared to horseradish peroxidase at pH 7.0. Assay of H2O2 was based on the measuring of absorbance at 652 nm, showing good linearity in the concentration range of 10 μM − 1 mM with a LOD of 10 μM. The probe exhibited several features such as small size, biocompatibility, and good solution, which favors to use them as ideal enzymatic mimics for various biomedical applications. A facile colorimetric miniaturized analytical platform was developed for the detection of H2O2 by using CoS1.035 NPs modified N-doped carbon framework porous dodecahedrons (CoS1.035 NPs/N-C PDHs) [S24]. The as-synthesized CoS1.035 NPs/N-C PDHs exhibited remarkable mimic peroxidase- and oxidase- like activities, thereby offering to investigate the peroxidase-based oxidative reaction of TMB in H2O2. The as-prepared CoS1.035NPs/N-C PDHs was efficiently catalyzed the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to yield a blue color, which results to appear absorbance at 652 nm. This method exhibited a wider linear range (0.5–120 μM) with a lower detection limit. Further, this method was also applied to detect glutathione (GSH), illustrating its promising applications for sensing of both H2O2 and GSH. Similar concept was also used for the detection of adenosine triphosphate via the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 [S25]. A novel nanocomposite Au-MnOx/imidazole(Im)@CNFs was introduced as a bifunctional biomimetic enzyme activity towards O2 •− and H2O2 sensing [S26]. The fabricated hybrid nanocomposites were used for the development of chips for real-time quantitative identification of both O2 •− and H2O2 species in cells. A novel inexpensive nanostructure material Fe3+ ion-inserted aminated lignin (Fe3+-AL) was used as a colorimetric probe for the detection of H2O2 [S27]. In this work, authors introduced FeNx as activate center into alkali lignin via Mannich or epoxidation reaction, thereby offering to act as Fe-AL nanozyme for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with a good linearity (10–100 mM). The developed probe exhibited good accuracy and precision in catalytic activity by using > 30 times.

To enhance peroxidase-like activity, reduced Co3O4 NPs have been introduced as a novel material for enhanced peroxidase-like activity [S28]. Authors investigated catalytic activities of two types of Co3O4 NPs (reduced-Co3O4 and pristine Co3O4 NPs), which reveals that reduced-Co3O4 NPs exhibited high affinity towards H2O2 as compared with reduced-Co3O4 NPs. The density functional theory (DFT) demonstrated that the peroxidase-like activity of reduced-Co3O4 NPs was greatly improved due to the introduction of oxygen vacancies to reduced-Co3O4 NPs, which favors to enhance H2O2 adsorption on reduced-Co3O4 NPs. As a result, this probe exhibited a wider linear range for the detection of H2O2 (1–30 μM) with an impressive LOD of 4.3 × 10–7 M. The developed analytical approach was successfully applied to detect glucose in human serum samples. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) was coated onto the surfaces of CuO nanoflowers for catalyze oxidation of TMB by H2O2 [S29]. The catalytic mechanism of PAN-CuO nanoflowers was investigated by using superoxide anion radical (O2 •–, scavenger p-benzoquinone), isopropyl alcohol or methyl alcohol (•OH scavenger) as reagents. These results demonstrated that PAN-CuO nanoflowers exhibited remarkable peroxidase-like activity, with offers to detect H2O2 even at 0.12 μM. Another approach illustrated that in-situ growth of Ag NPs onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for quantitative detection of H2O2 without need of coloring agent [S30]. The detection of H2O2 was based on the decrease of SPR band intensity of Ag NPs at 405 nm, which confirms the oxidation of Ag NPs by H2O2 (Ag0 NPs + 2 H2O2 → Ag+ ion + O2 •– + 2 H2O). The SPR band intensity was progressively increased as H2O2 concentration increased (0.1–100 μM) and the developed concept was further extended for sensing of glucose.

Recently, a new colorimetric sensor was developed for sensing of three target analytes including H2O2, glucose and L-cysteine using Cu-phthalocyanine- conjugated polymer NPs (Cu-PcCP NPs) as a probe [S31]. The steady-state kinetic studies revealed that peroxidase activity of Cu-PcCP NPs follows a typical Michaelis-Menten kinetic model. Another colorimetric sensing approach illustrated that the potential application of Cr2O3 NPs as a redox catalyst for H2O2 assay [S32]. The prepared nanosensor showed higher selectivity toward H2O2 than the other metal nanomaterials, exhibiting a simple analytical strategy for the detection of H2O2 in real samples (oxygenated water, oxidation cream, tulle/curtain bleaching detergent, triacetone triperoxide samples). The colorimetric assay of H2O2 was based on the reaction between Cr2O3 NPs and H2O2, favoring to estimate produced Cr6+ ions, which form complex with 1,5-diphenylcarbazide. Various nanostructure materials Fe3+ ion-doped CuSn(OH)6 microspheres [S33], polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP)- NiCo2S4 NPs [S34], mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA)-Cu NCs [S35], N-CDs [S36], Cr2O3–TiO2 [S37], core-shell Fe/FeS2 @C NPs [S38], FeS nanosheets [S39], iodide-Cu-metal organic-framework (MOF) [S40], MoOx QDs [S41], Ce2(WO4)3 nanosheets [S42] and cellulose nanowhiskers-Ag NPs [S43] have been used as promising materials for peroxidase-like activity for sensing of H2O2 in water, milk, normal and cancer cells. Kinetic parameters were studied and found that Fe3+ ion-doped CuSn(OH)6 microspheres and PVP-NiCo2S4 NPs exhibited peroxidase-like activity follows Michaelis–Menten kinetic model [S33-S34]. Catalytic mechanism was confirmed by ESR technique using 2,3-dimethylpyriodine-N-oxide (DMPO) as a probe [S34]. Interesting spectral characteristics were noticed in Cu NCs with low (0 – 0.1 mM) and high (0.5 – 1000 mM) concentrations of H2O2, yielding different colors (claret-red and red-yellow). The developed nanomaterials-based colorimetric probes were successfully applied to detect H2O2 in real samples (water, and milk) and in cell lines, showing good recoveries with low relative standard deviation (RSD) values. The above studies demonstrated that the current limitations (selectivity, rapidity and sensitivity) of traditional UV-Visible spectrometry for the detection of H2O2 are encountered when functional nanomaterials integrated with absorption spectrometry, thereby improving performance of UV–visible spectrometry for the detection of H2O2 in real samples with good accuracy. Thus, the above nanomaterials-based colorimetric methods have proven to be promising miniaturized analytical tools for assaying of H2O2 in real samples. Table 1 summarizes the nanomaterials-based methods for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 in environmental samples.

Hypochlorite (ClO–) has been recognized as one of the potential ROS, exhibiting significant effects on various biochemical pathways in cells [S44-S46]. In cells, ClO– can be generated by enzyme (heme enzyme myeloperoxidase) catalytic reaction where H2O2 and Cl- are involving as reagents. Due to its abnormal levels in the cells, it is also associated with various diseases including lung injury, atherosclerosis, neuron degeneration, cancer and cardiovascular diseases [S45]. Further, it is widely used as a potential oxidizer for various treatments such as deodorization, water disnfection and blanching. Thus, the combination of nanomaterials with UV-Visible spectroscopy offers impressive analytical platform for selective and sensitive recognition of ClO- in various samples. Citrate capped Au NPs were used for the hypochlorite detection [S47]. The sensing mechanism was based on the anti-aggregation, initially citrate-Au NPs were aggregated due to the addition of dithiothreitol (DTT), however, they will be segregated by the introduction of ClO-. The SPR peak of aggregated Au NPs was blue shifted with ClO-, which allows to visualize ClO- even at 2 μM within 5 min. The feasibility of the sensor was evaluated to assay ClO- ion water samples (tap, lake and natural water), exhibiting good recovery ranges from 97% to 110%, which can be used for sensing of hypochlorite. Similarly, cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB) was decorated on Ag NPs surfaces for colorimetric sensing of hypochlorite [43]. The addition of ClO- into CPB-Ag NPs was decreased the absorbance at ∼412 nm, indicating the aggregation of CPB-Ag NPs induced by hypochlorite, which yields a noticeable color change from yellow to colorless. The SPR peak intensity of CPB-Ag NPs was gradually decreased as hypochlorite concentration increased, showing a good linearity for the detection of ClO- with a LOD of 0.4538 μg mL−1. The selectivity of CPB-Ag NPs towards hypochlorite was investigated in the presence of wide variety of inorganic anions, exhibiting high selectivity towards hypochlorite ( Fig. 3). The developed method was efficiently applied to detect ClO- in water samples, showing good recoveries in the range of 98.80–102.94%. Triangular Ag nanoprisms (Ag NPRs) were synthesized and used as a colorimetric probe for the detection of hypochlorite [S48]. It was noticed that triangular Ag NPRs exhibited three SPR bands at 333, 475 and 716 nm, however the absorbance at 716 nm was decreased progressively with increasing amount of ClO-, followed by the gradual increase of SPR peak at 470 nm, which confirms that the conversion of morphology from Ag NPRs to spherical shape (Ag NPs). The quantification of ClO- was carried out by plotting calibration graph between shift in wavelength and concentration of ClO- (0.00–12.00 × 10−7 M) with a LOD of 2.50 × 10−10 M. This method was applied to detect ClO- in swimming pool and tap water, showing good recoveries from i 98.00–105.00%, signifying the practical application of the probe for sensing of ClO- without aid of sophisticated instrument.

Recent years, metal nanomaterials-based analytical methods have been recognized as a promising tool to recognize ClO- in biological and environmental samples. For example, Au nanospheres@Ag NRs [S49], UiO-68-PT MOF [S50], CDs [S51], GQDs [S52], B,N-doped CDs [S53] and green CDs [S54] have been used as colorimetric and fluorometric sensors for sensing of ClO- in cells and water samples. Interestingly, the LSPR band of Au nanospheres@Ag NRs was blue-shifted from 740 to 697, 652, 622, 578 and 539 nm with increasing concentration of hypochlorite, displaying a linear correlation between Δλmax and concentration of hypochlorite [S49]. The absorbances of CDs at 509 and 548 nm were gradually increased after the introduction of hypochlorite and decreased with ascorbic acid [S50]. The bright yellow color of CDs solution was changed to fuchsia with hypochlorite, however, it was recovered with the addition of ascorbic acid, which was used for both hypochlorite and ascorbic acid sensing. These approaches were successfully applied to assay hypochlorite in various water samples, displaying good recoveries in the range of > 90 - > 100% with lower RSD values. Further, the concentration of hypochlorite in cells was successfully estimated using CDs as a dual probe (colorimetric and fluorometric sensors). Very recently, magneto-chiral Co(OH)2 NPs were successfully synthesized and showed a remarkable unique magnetic and chiroptical properties [S55]. The as-prepared Co(OH)2 NPs acted as a probe to recognize and monitor ClO- in cells and in vivo. The Co(OH)2 NPs were integrated with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and circular dichroism techniques for the detection of intra cellular ClO-, exhibiting an impressive LOD at pM/106 cells with a wider linear range 0.673–612.971 pM/106 cells. Noticeably, Co(OH)2 NPs have shown high ability to assay the levels of ClO- in mice by MRI and fluorescence microscopy. These reports revealed that functional nanostructure materials can be used as promising materials for the development of facile colorimetric analytical methods for monitoring of hypochlorite in living cells and environmental samples. With the modification of nanomaterials surface, a facile and sensitive detection of ClO- (nM to pM) was achieved. The surface modified nanostructures showed improved selectivity and sensitivity as compared to unmodified nanomaterials, thereby allowing them to use as potential probes for the detection of ClO- in biological objects.

Superoxide dismutase (SOD) is considered as one of the metalloenzyme with antioxidant property that shows a key role in microorganisms, animals and plants [S56]. The enzyme acts as a good therapeutic candidate against ROS-mediated diseases via protecting cell damages by ROS [S57]. Catalase abnormal (excess or low) H2O2, and SOD is an oxidoreductase that serves to dismutate O2 •− [S58]. Moreover, SOD catalyzes the removal of O2 •− by dismutating it into O2 and H2O2, which results possible elimination of HO• formation via Haber-Weiss reaction [S59]. Based on the metal ion binding to SOD, SOD is categorized into three isozymes: Mn-SOD (localized in mitochondria), Cu/Zn-SOD (localized in cytosol, peroxisomes, and chloroplasts), Ni-SOD and Fe-SOD (localized in chloroplasts) [S60]. Due to its key roles, recently, few nanomaterials have been introduced as colorimetric sensors for the detection of SOD in real samples. Citrate-Au NPs acted as colorimetric sensor for selective recognition of temporal evolution of SOD1 in clinical samples [44]. It was observed that the SPR band and color of Au NPs showed significant changes with the addition of SOD1. UV–visible spectroscopic studies demonstrated that the SPR spectra of Au NPs-SOD1 was significantly red-shifted from 526 to 700 nm ( Fig. 4) with increasing time from day 1 to day 4. The characteristic SPR band at 526 nm was completely disappeared and red-shifted, signifying the continuous growth of aggregation of Au NPs induced by SOD1, which yields a change in the color of Au NPs from red-purple-bluish-purple-blue (Fig. 4b). Further, Au NPs were coated on thin-layer chromatography plates and then treated with SOD1, displaying noticeable color changes at different time intervals. The Au NPs-SOD1 aggregates have shown high ability to interact with SOD1 monomer, favoring to establish a facile analytical tool for the detection of structural evolution via UV–visible spectroscopy.

Further, Pt-decorated g-C3N4 nanostructures were used as a photocatalytic colorimetric probe for visual read-out assay of SOD [45]. This colorimetric assay was based on the oxidation of dye by the generated O2 •− via photocatalytic activity of Pt-inserted g-C3N4, which results to yield colorless end product. Among several dyes, azo dye amaranth has found to be suitable color indicator for the identification of O2 •−. The characteristic absorbance of Pt-inserted g-C3N4 at 522 nm was decreased steadily with the increasing levels of SOD, allowing the visual readout assay of O2 •− event at 0.3 nM with a wider linear range (1.5–4500 nM). Authors used different LED colors and showed that the violet LED (380–390 nm) exhibited best photo-oxidation process ( Fig. 5a). Interestingly, triethanolamine, tryptophan, p-benzoquinone and isopropanol acted as h+, 1O2, O2 •− and .OH radicals (Fig. 5b). Pt-doped g-C3N4 NSs have ability to generate O2 •− whereas H2O2 and O2 are generated by catalytic reaction of SOD as dismutase (Fig. 5c). Vanadium nitride NPs coated on the carbon nanofibers (VN NPs@CNFs) using electrospinning method via ammonia reduction [S61]. The as-fabricated VN NPs@CNFs exhibited outstanding cascade enzyme mimics (superoxide dismutase and peroxidase (POD)- like activities). Superoxide anion was produced by the reaction between riboflavin and methionine under UV light. The absorbance spectra showed peak at 560 nm due to the reaction of nitroblue tetrazole with O2 •−, resulting to form blue methyl hydrazone. However, the formation of blue methyl hydrazone can be minimized by consuming O2 •− with the use of nanostructure materials (SOD-like activity). To support this, the color intensity and absorbance at 560 nm were remarkably decreased by the addition of VN NPs@CNFs nanocomposites, confirming the SOD-like activity. To confirm the peroxidase activity, the absorption spectra of TMB were measured separately by adding VN NPs/TMB/H2O2, CNFs/TMB/H2O2 and VN NPs@CNFs/TMB/H2O2 systems. It was noticed that the strong absorbance at 652 nm was noticed only with VN NPs@CNFs/TMB/H2O2 system, revealing the oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB by ROS, which signifies the peroxidase-like activity of VN NPs@CNFs. The developed method exhibited promising colorimetric sensor for the rapid and sensitive monitoring of superoxide anion with a LOD of 0.0167 μM. The developed method was effectively applied to detect SOD in serum samples with good precision and accuracy. A novel AuNPs-MnOx/Im@CNFs hybrid nanocomposite was used as a probe for the detection of two ROS (O2 •− and H2O2) in Hela and 293 A cells [S26]. To examine the practical application of method, sensing chip was fabricated with AuNPs-MnOx/Im@ACNFs and studied their electrochemical sensing abilities by bending chip at different angles (0°, 60°, 90°, 120° and 180°). These results demonstrated that H2O2 was successfully detected due to the unique properties of AuNPs-MnOx/Im@ACNFs, revealing prepared hybrid chip exhibited good flexibility due to acidified carbon nanofibers (ACNFs) high surface to volume ratio and remarkable mechanical flexibility. In addition, imidazole (Im) was effectively promoted structural integrity of hybrid nanocomposite by inducing strong interface between AuNPs, MnOx and ACNFs. Chronoamperometry was used to investigate sensing ability of AuNPs-MnOx/Im@ACNFs chip towards H2O2 and O2 .-. Based on the obtained current responses, it was confirmed that HeLa cells are released high amounts of ROS than that of normal 293 A cells, which signifies the feasibility of nanocomposite-based sensing chip for quantitative detection of ROS in cells.

Influence of surface ligands (citrate and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)) was investigated on the formation of Au NPs and Au NRs for colorimetric sensing of O2 •− [S62]. It was observed that CTAB-Au NRs showed superior characteristics as compared to citrate-Au NPs. The CTAB-Au NRs showed two LSPR bands at 640 and 520 nm and sensing ability was investigated by the addition of Fe2+, H2O2 and KSCN, leading a blue-shift (640–520 nm), which results to a decrease aspect ratio due to the deformation of LSPR band. This results also confirmed that the formation of O2 •− that is produced by the reaction of H2O2 with Fe2+ ion. Based on this, a colorimetric O2 •− assay was carried out by UV–visible spectroscopy using CTAB-Au NRs as a probe, showing a significant LSPR shift to longer wavelength progressively with increasing levels of SOD, with an impressive LOD of 0.25 U. Authors extended the application of CTAB-Au NRs for recognition of SOD by preparing CTAB-Au NRs-based hydrogels, where the levels of SOD are effectively visualized with naked-eye. The developed CTAB-Au NRs-based colorimetric assay was applied to detect SOD in fetal bovine serum samples (4%), showing good reproducibility and repeatability for assaying of SOD. Recently, a novel dual probe (fluorescence and colorimetry) was developed for parallel sensing of H2O2, O2 •− and Fe2+ ion using CDs functionalized CdTe QDs [S63]. The degradation of CDs-CdTe QDs was noticed in the presence of •OH, resulting a decrease in the absorption at 500 nm. Noticeably, a deep color solution was formed by the addition of H2O2 where Fe2+ ion reacts with –NH2 and –COOH groups of CDs, which results to form a complex. As a result, a significant increase in the absorption at 360 nm, displaying a good linearity in the •OH concentration (0.01 −0.1 μM, R
2 =0.997), with a LOD of 8 nM. The practical application of CDs-CdTe QDs-based assay was successfully applied to detect both H2O2 and Fe2+ in urine and serum samples. These results demonstrated that the unique features of nanostructure materials have improved the analytical performance of a simple UV–visible spectroscopy just like as sophisticated analytical tools, which offers innovative miniaturized analytical methods for sensing of ROS in biological and environmental samples without need of specific analytical tool or sample preparation. Table 2 overviews the integration of various types of nanomaterials as sensors for colorimetric detection of ClO- and superoxide dismutase in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing of ROS
ce:section.ce:para: The structural and optical features of various nanostructure materials endow them as ideal probes for the recognition of wide variety of chemical and biomolecules [46–48]. Recently, significant increasing popularity has been received by various fluorescent nanostructure materials in analytical chemistry [49–52]. The molecular assembly on the surfaces of nanostructure materials make facile route to fabricate specific target molecular system, which favors nanostructure materials to function as fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of various analytes at trace level. Further, fluorescent nanostructure materials have been used as recognition elements for the detection of various ROS. The extremely short lifetime of ROS, as well as the wide range of antioxidants that exist in-vivo capable of capturing these ROS, make them difficult to detect. However, the research survey found that the fluorescence methods have shown remarkable ability to detect ROS in in-situ or at generation source, among other techniques. Because of the high spatial and temporal resolution [S64], decreased metastatic spread, extremely good sensitivity and selectivity with a rapid response [19,S65], fluorescence methods are considered as effective analytical tool for revealing and detection of various molecules, especially ROS sensing. This section overviews the detection of ROS using functional nanomaterials as fluorescent probes. Scheme 2 demonstrates that the nanomaterials as fluorescent probes for the identification of ROS is real samples.

Functional nanomaterials-based fluorescence methods have shown high spatiotemporal resolution and very sensitivity for the detection of H2O2. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) capped Cu NCs were synthesized by Ling et al. and used as a nanosensor for the detection of H2O2 [S66]. PEI-decorated Cu NCs show a blue emission at 480 nm with an average diameter of 1.8 nm. In the presence of H2O2, the emission intensity of Cu NCs is quenched. The PEI-capped Cu NCs exhibited stable and strong fluorescence emission intensity at pH in the range of 3.3–9.0. The quantitative detection of H2O2 was achieved in the range of 0.5– 10 × 10−6 M with a LOD of 0.4 × 10−6 M. A label-free fluorescence analytical method was developed for the detection of H2O2 in serum sample using poly(thymine) single strand DNA-templated Cu NCs as a probe [S67]. In this approach, H2O2 was quantified by constructing calibration graph between the emission intensity quenching (λEm=598 nm and λEx= 345 nm) and the concentration of H2O2 (1–30 μM), displaying a LOD of 0.5 μM. This method was applied to quantify H2O2 in spiked serum samples with good recoveries.

Recently, metal NCs including Au NCs [53, S68-S70] and Ag NCs [S71] have been used as sensors for sensing of H2O2 in environmental, cells and biofluids. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [S68] and BSA proteins functionalized on the surface of Au NCs and used for the quantification of H2O2 in liver cells [53]. HRP was denatured in the presence of H2O2, resulting to break Au-S bond, which yields a drastic quenching in the emission peak of Au NCs. The intensity was decreased linearly with the addition of H2O2 (100 × 10−9 – 100 × 10−6 M) with a LOD of 30 × 10−9 M [S68]. A novel fluorescence analytical concept was developed for the quantification of H2O2 in liver cells by using BSA-Au NCs [53]. Auto-generation of O2 is not possible with only H2O2 or only BSA-Au NCs, but oxygen was generated by mixing both via the degradation of H2O2. In this work, H2O2 was generated with help of 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole and then accumulated into WRL-68 cells. BSA-Au NCs were treated with WRL-68 cells, resulting the quenching of red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs by the accumulation of H2O2. It was observed that the red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs was linearly quenched with increasing amount of H2O2, yielding a change in the red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs to non-fluorescent ( Fig. 6a). Based on this, authors quantified H2O2 by plotting linear graph between quenching emission intensity and concentrations of H2O2 (0.001–50 mM) (Fig. 6b), with a LOD of 0.7 μM. Inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism was established for sensing of glucose and H2O2 using histidine-Au NCs [S69]. It was noticed that AuNPs acted as glucose oxidase (GOx)-mimicking catalyst to oxidize glucose for in-situ generation of H2O2, which leads to enlarge SPR spectra of Au NPs with increasing amount of glucose or H2O2. The emission intensity of histidine-Au NPs was remarkably quenched due to overlap between the SPR spectrum of Au NPs (absorber) and emission spectrum of histidine-Au NCs) (fluorophore) via IFE mechanism as the levels of glucose or H2O2 increased. This method exhibited good linearity with H2O2 concentration, showing a LOD of 3.6 μM. For the detection of H2O2 in HeLa cell, the cells were stimulated with phorbol myristate acetate (5 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 5 h and the levels of H2O2 was estimated by using CDs-Au NCs-based fluorescence method [S70].

A dule mode (fluorometric- colorimetric) nanoprobe was developed for sensing of H2O2 by using MoS2 NSs with ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD) [54]. When H2O2 was treated with OPD-MoS2 NSs, the color of solution was changed from pale yellow to yellow, showing λmax and λEx/Em at 450 and 450/557 nm, which confirms the oxidation of OPD. The method has ability to recognize H2O2 event at 500 nM, displaying a good linearity in the range of 5.5 – 12.6 μM for H2O2 at HeLa cells (102–106 cells). Another a dual sensor was developed for the detection of hydrogen peroxide using TPE-SiO2 @Ag NPs as a probe [S11]. The fluorescence detection of H2O2 was based on AIE mechanism, allowing to detect H2O2 in human serum and intracellular imaging of H2O2. Similarly, Ru3+ ion-decorated C3N4 NSs exhibited peroxidase mimicking nature by catalyzing the oxidation of OPD to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (2,3-DAP) in the presence of H2O2 [55]. The formed 2,3-DAP was effectively quenched the emission peak of Ru3+ ion-decorated C3N4 NSs via IFE mechanism. BSA was used as a ligand for synthesis of stable and water dispersible Zn/Cu NCs for the reaction of H2O2 and NaHCO3 [56]. A strong CL was noticed due to the decomposition of both BSA-Zn/Cu NCs and HCO4 –, leading to drastic changes in BSA, which yields the aggregation of BSA-Zn/Cu NCs. This method showed good linear range from 5 × 10−9 to 1 × 10−6 M, with a LOD of 3 × 10−10 M. A fluorescence assay of H2O2 was carried out by Fenton reaction-stimulated fluorescence quenching of N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) -Au NCs [57]. This method was also used for the detection of glucose in human serum, which showed a LOD of 0.027 μM.

Recently, Ce3+ ion was decorated on ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs for ratiometric fluorescence detection of three ROS (H2O2, ClO-, and ONOO-) in RAW264.7 and HeLa cells [58]. It was observed that Ce3+ ion was changed to Ce4+ in the presence of ROS, signifying phosphatase-like catalytic activity, which results to generate blue emission from 4-methylumbelliferone due to the dephosphorylation of 4-methylumbelliferyl phosphate (non-fluorescent). ROS showed negligible impact on the emission peak of ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs at 630 nm (red emission). The emission peak at 450 nm was progressively increased with increasing amounts of ROS in the presence of Ce3+ ion. This concept was successfully applied to detect ROS in RAW264.7 and HeLa cells. Another report illustrated the promising application of porous CuO NCs for specific tumor cell targets [59]. In this approach, oleylamine was assembled onto the surface of CuO NCs and then functionalized with folic acid, thereby effectively catalyze hydrogen peroxide in cell environment. Similarly, blue fluorescence chitosan-Cu NCs were prepared and explored as nanosensors for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of ROS (ClO- and•OH) and “turn-on” detection of antioxidants (GSH and ascorbic acid) [60]. The sensing of ROS was based on the quenching of emission peak of Cu NCs in the presence of ClO- (36 μM) and •OH (12.5 μM), respectively. The practical applications of the method was demonstrated by assaying ClO- in water samples. A ratiometric fluorescence method was developed for the detection of ROS (∙OH, ONOO− and ClO−) using cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)-capped Au NCs [61]. Fig. 7 illustrates the schematic representation for the fabrication of CNC-Au NCs for radiometric fluorescence sensing of ROS in cells. The ratiometric fluorescence behavior of CNC-Au NCs was investigated with the addition of ClO−, resulting a linear decrease in the emission peak of CNC-Au NCs at 488 nm and a linear increase in the emission peak of OPD at 580 nm. Yellow emission was observed due to oxidation of OPD (non-fluorescent) to 2,3-DAP (yellow fluorescence) in the presence of ROS. The excitation wavelengths of CNC-Au NCs and OPD are overlapped at 350 nm ∼ 410 nm, however the emission wavelengths of CNC-Au NCs and 2,3-DAP are well separated (λEm 488 nm and λEm 580 nm) ( Fig. 8a). The emission intensity at 488 nm was gradually decreased with ClO− concentration, showing wider detection range from 0 to 400 μmol/L (Fig. 8b-c). Additionally, the emission peak at 580 nm (OPD) was progressively increased with increasing amounts of ClO− (Fig. 8d). The developed ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibited remarkable sensitivity and selectivity in sensing of ROS, offering LODs of 0.87, 0.97 and 0.79 μM/L for ONOO−, ClO−, and ·OH, respectively. Further, CNC-Au NCs acted as promising biocompatibility ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of ROS both in-vivo and in-vitro, illustrating their efficacy for assaying of ROS in living cells. Polymeric fluorescent nanoparticles have been used as fluorescent sensors for the detection of hydroxyl radical in HEI-OC1 cells [62]. According to the studies, the difference in the emission peak in the presence of ROS revealed that fluorescent nanomaterials could be used as promising probes for ROS assay in tissues and bifluids, which also favors to use them as a naked-eye sensor for the detection of ROS including H2O2 in real samples.

Hydroxyl radicals are the neutral form of •HO ion and shown high ability to react with chemical species, which cause severe damage to various cells in the body [63]. Zhang et. al. developed a label-free fluorescent sensor for monitoring of •OH radicals using DNA-Ag NCs as a probe [64]. The as-prepared DNA-Ag NCs showed λmax at 397 nm and λEx/Em at 433 and 550 nm. The assay of •OH radicals was performed by Fenton reaction where Fe2+ ion can react with hydrogen peroxide. It was noticed that no significant change was observed in the emission peak of Ag NCs at 550 nm with independent addition of Fe2+ ion and H2O2. However, the emission peak at 550 nm was linearly decreased with the addition of both H2O2 and Fe2+ ions simultaneously, showing wider linear range in the concentration of H2O2 (50 nM - 5.0 μM). Similarly, BSA-Ag NCs were introduced as fluorescent reader for sensing of ROS (H2O2, •OH and O2 •-) and expanded the approach for the investigation of specific relationship between ROS, and development of mechanism of cell adhesion [65]. Further, bioimaging of •OH was successfully carried out by using BSA-Au NCs and further modification with 2-[6-(4′-hydroxy) phenoxy-3 H-xanthen-3-on-9-yl] benzoic acid (HPF) [66]. The fabricated BSA-HPF-Au NCs acted as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of hydroxy free radical. When excited at 488 nm, Au NCs display the emission peak at 637 nm, while the addition of •OH induced the oxidative dearylation of HPF, which results to produce the emission peak at 515 nm. The as-prepared BSA-HPF-Au NCs exhibited remarkable selectivity toward •OH over other ROS, and metal ions, offering impressive LOD of ∼0.68 μM. The probe was applied as biocompatible ratiometric nanosensor for monitoring of •OH levels in living cell systems upon oxidative stress. The GSH was used as surface ligand for functionalization of Au NCs for sensing of ROS including ONOO−, •OH and ClO−, respectively [67]. To improve the practical utility of Au NCs, oligopeptides and N,N,N-trimethyl-(11-mercaptaundecyl)ammonium chloride were used as surface ligands for further modulate Au NCs surface chemistry, thereby improving an effective cellular uptake and monitoring of ROS in cells and zebrafish. Surface modified metal NCs acted as promising fluorescent agents for monitoring of ROS in living systems. Fluorescent nanomaterials-based sensors offer significant advantages such as rapidity, selectivity and sensitivity for ROS assay in cells over conventional analytical methods.

It is well known that ClO- is widely used chlorinating (bleaching or disinfectant) agent for various treatments. Further, it has been utilized as promising agent for the treatment of cyanide containing wastewater. In view of this, nanostructured materials Si QDs [68], Au-Ag NCs [69,70], MoS2 QDs [71], N,F-CDs [72], Au(I)-Ag(I)-Cys nanoplatelets [73], upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs)- polyacrylic acid (PAA)-Rh1000 dye [74], sea urchin-like carbon nanobranched polymers (SUCNPs) [75], NH2-Cu-MOFs [76], UCNPs (NaGdF4: Yb3+ and Er3+@NaYF4) treated - Astrazon Brilliant Red 4 G (ABR 4 G) [77], Ti3C2 MXene QDs [78], 1, 2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-poly(ethylene glycol) (DSPE- PEG)-capped Au NCs [79], purple-red carbon spheres [80] and N-doped CDs [81–83] have been integrated with fluorescence spectrometry for assaying and bioimaging of ClO- in real samples. Blue fluorescent Si QDs were prepared by hydrothermal approach and applied as a probe for sensing of ClO- in tap water [68]. Bimetallic Au-Ag NCs acted as fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of ClO- in water and lysozymes [69,70]. BSA-Au-Ag NCs showed an intense emission peak at 610 nm and significantly quenched with the addition of ClO- ion. The developed sensor showed satisfactory quantitative recoveries (93.5–106.5%) for the determination of ClO- ion in water samples. A facile single step reaction was introduced for synthesis of water soluble cysteine-Au(I)–Ag(I) nanoplatelets for monitoring of intracellular ClO- in HeLa cells [69]. Blue fluorescent water soluble MoS2 QDs were fabricated for fluorescence sensing of hypochlorite in water samples [71]. The fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs was linearly quenched with increasing concentration of hypochlorite (5–500 μM) and exhibited linear Stern-Volmer plot, which achieved LOD of 0.5 μM. Glycine and 4-fluorophthalic acid were used as precursors for fabrication of N-F-co-doped CDs via hydrothermal method and applied as rapid fluorescent sensor for sensing of ClO- in piped water [72]. This probe showed wider linearity in the range of 0–35 μM, with a LOD of 9.6 nM.

Hypochlorite-responsive fluorescent probe was developed by the combination of UCNPs with Rh1000 dye [74]. The detection of ClO- was due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism and efficiently monitored ClO- levels in vitro. A simple solvothermal approach was introduced for the synthesis of SUCNPs and used as a probe for monitoring of ClO- ion in real samples water [75]. The quantitative assay of ClO- was performed by plotting calibration graph between the ratio (I401/436) and ClO- (0.1–200 μM) [75]. Noticeably, the lifetime of SUCNPs was decreased from 9.08 ns to 5.34 ns with ClO− ion, revealing the surface defects were significantly influenced by ClO- ion, which confirms that the detection was based on photoinduced charge transfer (PCT) process. Further, NH2-Cu-MOFs acted as a nanoprobe for the detection of ClO- via static quenching mechanism [76]. The developed NH2-Cu-MOFs-based fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity towards ClO- over other ROS species. Hypochlorite was successfully detected by developing by UCNPs-ABR 4 G as a probe [77]. In this approach, ABR-4 G was quenched the green emission of UCNPs, however it was restored by introducing ClO- ion. This strategy was applied to assay ClO- in biofluids and industrial wastewater. FRET process was noticed between Ti3C2 MXene QDs and curcumin, resulting to quench emission peak of Ti3C2 MXene QDs [78]. Interestingly, the emission peak of Ti3C2 MXene QDs was linearly restored with the addition of ClO- ion, favoring to develop visual read-out strategy for sensing of ClO- ion.

Purple-red carbon sphere acted as a dual (colorimetric and fluorometric) probe for the detection of ClO- via energy transfer process [79]. Under optimal conditions, the probe exhibited good linearity between the emission intensity and hypochlorite (0–90 μM) with a LOD of 0.13 μM. The probe was applied to assay ClO- in tap water. Similarly, N-doped CDs [81–83] were introduced as biocompatible fluorescent nanosensor for monitoring of ClO- in cells and water samples. This method was exhibited good linearity for quantitative detection of ClO-, which offers an impressive LOD of 0.43 μM and successfully used to assay ClO- in swimming pool and tap water. Green fluorescent CDs were effectively decorated onto MoO3 NPs surfaces for sensing of ClO- in various water samples [84]. With the increasing of ClO- concentration (0.010–200 µM), the emission peak of green-CDs-MoO3 NPs was linearly quenched ( Fig. 9), achieving remarkable LOD of 6.8 nM for ClO-, which revealed that the developed probe exhibited promising analytical features for assaying of ClO-. These reports demonstrated that nanostructure materials-based fluorescent probes are considered to be rapid and miniaturized sensing approaches for monitoring of hypochlorite in water samples.

Superoxide anion (O2 •–) is one of ROS and involving significant roles in cell metabolism and growth [S72]. At the same time excess levels of O2 •– cause oxidative stress in living cells, indicating in situ detection of O2 •– plays an important role for investigation of disease diagnosis and pathological process. Briefly, poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA)– and BSA capped Cu NCs were prepared separately for the development of dual emission nanosensor for monitoring of hydroxyl and superoxide anion [85]. In order to ratiometric fluorescence detection, PMAA-Cu NCs and BSA-Cu NCs were mixed and showed emission peaks at 445 nm and 652 nm when λEx at 360 nm. The emission peak at 445 nm was progressively increased with the addition of both ROS (•OH and O2 •−). Interestingly, the emission peak at 652 nm was linearly quenched with •OH, while no change with O2 •−, favoring to develop ratiometric fluorescence method for the detection of both ROS. The developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibited LODs of 1.8 and 0.15 μM for O2 •− and •OH, respectively, which was effectively applied to quantify both ROS in in L-132 cells. Further, Au NPs-BSA- terbium/guanosine monophosphate disodium (Tb/GMP) was explored as a ratiometric probe for monitoring of O2 •− [86]. The fabricated Au NPs-BSA-Tb/GMP acted as a selective nanosensor for the quantification of O2 •− with wider linearity in the range of 14 nM – 10 μM, with a LOD of 4.7 nM. Several fluorescent nanomaterials such as fluorescein isothiocyanate(FITC)-mSiO2-hmSiO2 [87], Ag NPs in Ag NCs-CDs [88], BSA-Au NPs complexed with Te-CDs [89] and Fe3+ ion-CDs [90] have been efficiently used as fluorescent probes for sensing and imaging of O2 •− in living cells. Briefly, green fluorescence of fluorescein isothiocyanate (518 nm) was stopped by introducing hydroethidine in the presence of O2 •–, yielding red fluorescence at 570 nm due to the oxidation of hydroethidine by O2 •– [87].

Fluorescent nanocomposite was prepared by functionalizing Ag NPs on CDs, resulting to form core-shell Ag NCs@CDs for imaging of O2 •– MCF-7 cells [88]. In this approach, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 440 nm was quenched by Ag NPs in Ag NCs-CDs, however the emission peak at 440 was restored with the addition of O2 •– due to the oxidation of Ag NPs, resulting a good linearity between the ratio of fluorescence intensity and O2 •– concentration (0.6 −1.6 μM), with a LOD 0.3 μM. This approach was successfully demonstrated for imaging of O2 •− in MCF-7 cells. Another report illustrated the utility of BSA-Au NPs complexed with Te-CDs for monitoring of O2 •– levels in living systems [89]. As a result, a simple analytical strategy was developed for the ratiometric detection of O2 •– with wider linear range from 0.2 to 20 μM, favoring to detect O2 •– even at 80 nM. Noticeably, the probe exhibited two emission peaks at 640 and 425 nm with the addition of O2 •–, revealing its ability to in situ imaging, and scavenging of O2 •– in various cells. Similarly, the fluorescence of CDs was remarkably decreased by specifically Fe3+ ion due to energy transfer mechanism via the formation of Fe3+ ion-CDs complex [90]. Noticeably, the quenched fluorescence intensity at 556 nm was linearly recovered with increasing amounts of O2 •− (0.3–2.4 nM) ( Fig. 10a), displaying good linearity for the quantification of O2 •− with impressive LOD of 25 pM. In order to develop paper-based analytical approach, various concentration of O2 •− (2 – 12 μM) were placed on paper and then treated with probe solution for few seconds. To this, 30 μL of different amounts of O2 •− was added onto the paper and the paper was placed under UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 10b), displaying significant fluorescence colors, which could be observed with naked-eye. These results revealed that this probe could be used as promising analytical approach for on-site monitoring and instant sensing of O2 •− ion biological and environmental samples.

Peroxynitrite (ONOO-), is a reactive nitrogen species that formed by the quick reaction between nitric oxide and O2 •– in living cells, where it easily induces irreversible harm to lipids, proteins, and DNA [91]. As a result, unmanaged ONOO- production has been linked to a variety of pathogens, such as ischemia/reperfusion injury, neurodegeneration, inﬂammatory, and cancer. Development of a sensing method for the preferential cellular identification of ONOO- in the existence of other ROS/RNS may possibly enhance analytic advances, contributing to enhanced therapies for a variety of health conditions [92]. In comparison with other ROS, ONOO- can spontaneously decompose into a series of radicals. Thus, understanding of the role of ONOO- and its accurate determination will provide insight into viral pathogenesis at a molecular level.

Several fluorescent-based nanomaterials CdTe and CdTe@ZnS QDs capped with GSH, 3-mercaptopropionicacid (MPA), or thioglycolic acid (TGA) [93], ferrocene- CDs [94], benzoperylene-Mn2+ ion-ZnS QDs [95], CdTe QDs [96], Hf-based UiO-66-B (OH)2 MOF [97], Au NCs-SiO2 [98] and phycocyanin-CDs [99] have been designed as selective and sensitive probes for sensing of ONOO- in various cells. Authors investigated influence of capping agents MPA, TGA and GSH on the surfaces of CdTe and CdTe-ZnS QDs for sensing of ONOO- [93]. It was noticed that MPA-TGA-CdTe-ZnS QDs exhibited superior sensitivity towards ONOO- as compared other ligand systems. Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism was developed for the detection of ONOO- using ferrocene-CDs as a nanosensor [94]. Experimental and analytical data illustrated that PET process was due to electron transfer from ferrocene to CDs, resulting the quenching of emission peak, which shows good linearity in the concentration range of 4 nM - 0.12 μM with a LOD of 2.9 nM. Importantly, intracellular ONOO− levels were effectively monitored in MCF-7 cells by fluorescence microscopy using ferrocene-CDs. The combination of benzoperylene and Mn2+-ZnS QDs was studied for the generation of nanoprobe with dual emission nature for sensing of ONOO- [95]. It was observed that probe was very sensitive towards ROS, showing drastic quenching with the addition of ROS. To generate CL, cells loaded onto CdTe QDs for CL sensing of ONOO- selectively in living cells [96]. The developed probe showed remarkable selectivity towards ONOO- even in the presence of other ROS (O2 •−, •OH, ClO– and H2O2) and applied as specific CL probe for monitoring of ONOO- in HeLa cells. Selective sensing of ONOO- was carried out by using boronic acid-Hf-based UiO-66 MOF as a fluorescent probe [97]. The prepared fluorescent probe showed non-toxic to cells and used for imaging of ONOO- in cells. Another report described that the use of dye-encapsulated SiO2 NPs-Au NCs as a dual emission probe for imaging of ROS including ONOO- in RAW 264.7 cells [98]. The fluorescence of designed dual emission probe was selectively quenched by ROS and developed calibration graphs between ratio of emission intensities and concentration of ROS (0.03–40, 0.5–100 and 0.2–90 μM for •OH, ClO- and ONOO-, respectively), offering to monitor above ROS in RAW 264.7 cells with high selectivity. Recently, phycocyanin functionalized CDs were used as a nanosensor for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ONOO- in real samples [99]. The as-prepared phycocyanin-CDs showed extraordinary selectivity for ONOO- over other ROS, and successfully applied to quantify ONOO- in the range of 0.5–100 μM with a LOD of 0.5 μM, displaying good recoveries 99% − 104% from phosphate buffered saline, which allows to use as simple fluorescent probe for monitoring of ONOO- in environmental and biological samples. Table 3 illustrates the sensing applications of nanomaterials for fluorescence detection of ROS in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and perspectives of nanosensors for ROS assay
ce:section.ce:para: ROS (H2O2, O2 •−, •OH, ClO- and ONOO−) serve as powerful antimicrobial agents, which are produced through incomplete reduction of oxygen species. In recent decades, nanomaterials combined with optical spectroscopic methods are widely used for the optical sensing/quantification of ROS in various reactions, because of their unique optical, simple cellular and subcellular targeting, stable, and physical properties such as permit tunable interactions with cells and media with enable ratiometric sensing properties of nanomaterials. Nanomaterials (metal NPs, CDs, C3N4, QDs, metal oxides, and hybrid nanostructures)-based optical sensors demonstrated several noteworthy advantages, including fast, simplicity, sensitivity, and selectivity, which favors improving the sensitivity and selectivity of optical spectrometry, and proven to be promising miniaturized analytical method for identification of variety of ROS. However, due to high spatial and temporal resolution, low metastatic spread, rapid response with high sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence techniques combined with nanomaterials are considered as effective optical sensing method for ROS compared with other conventional methods was conclude from this survey. Despite remarkable achievements in nanomaterials-based optical sensor development, it is still new and young with many challenges and as well as opportunities. For example, the synthesis and design of stable and biocompatible nanomaterials-based optical sensing analytical methods for ROS in biological systems both in vitro and in vivo is yet to be explore. Furthermore, to design or develop novel nanomaterial-based sensors for ROS, it is need to understand the mechanisms or interactions in deep. To develop miniaturized or digitalized nanomaterial-based fluorescing analytical device, further investigations are need to explore in this area. Furthermore, research into improving the spatial resolution of ROS and oxygen localization using photoacoustic and near infrared-II dyes has showed significant promise in better depth profiling, where a better understanding of illness development might benefit in potential and future therapy.

This survey proven that the most of the peroxide radicals detected by colorimetric techniques using metal oxides/magnetic particles modified carbon materials such as graphitic carbon nitrite, carbon nanotubes and other carbon materials. However, the cytotoxicity and real field application feasibility of this materials further need to be explored. Moreover, the use these materials in other analytical techniques not yet be explored. Most of ROS were identified and quantified using various nanomaterials (Ag NPs, QDs, metal oxides and hybrid materials), however, the detection of trace levels of ROS, re-usability and sustainability of nanomaterials was questionable. Even though remarkable achievements have been achieved for monitoring of ROS by functional nanomaterials, nanomaterials-based absorption and fluorescence spectrometric methods are unable to meet the commercial and practical applications due to their reproducibility in large scale preparations and requirement of specific chemicals. Further, toxicity of ROS nanosensor has been assessed by studying cytotoxicity on one cell line as an example, but this not sufficient to define biocompatibility of nanomaterials in biological systems. To explore nanomaterials as practical ROS sensors, the following challenges still need to be addressed (i) deep and long-term investigations are required for transforming of ROS nanosensors from lab to commercial analytical tool kit, (ii) fabrication of ROS nanosensors with simple, inexpensive and label-free synthetic approaches and (iii) designing of ideal ROS nanosensors with reproducible optical characteristics and good quantum yields, which improves their use for monitoring of ROS in vivo and in vitro studies. Further, significant efforts must be focused on the fabrication of nanomaterials as reusable ROS nanosensors, thereby minimizing synthetic procedures, and hazardous materials. Moreover, investigations mush be focused on the coupling of ROS nanosensors with smart-phone for the development of user friendly miniaturized analytical device for environmental monitoring applications. Overall, coupling of nanomaterials with absorption and fluorescence spectrometric techniques has greatly improved the analytical features for ROS sensing in real samples, making them as good candidates for sensing of ROS in real samples. Based on the achieved analytical merits, we expect the integration of nanomaterials with absorption and fluorescence spectrometric techniques for ROS detection and imaging will remain exciting and hot research area in environmental and biomedical research.

In this review, the potential use of nanomaterials as colorimetric and fluorometric probes for sensing and imaging of ROS (H2O2, •OH, O2 •−, ONOO−) in environmental, biological and living cells are summarized. Colorimetric and fluorometric detections were successfully carried out by using various nanomaterials including metal NPs (Au, Ag and Cu), metal NCs (Au, Ag and Cu), g-C3N4, metal oxides, CDs, MOFs and core-shell nanostructures as probes. Surface modification and doping of metal ions hetero atoms are greatly improved the profiles of nanoprobes for selective monitoring of ROS in environmental samples and their in situ imaging in various cells. It was also interestingly noticed that ROS sensing nanoprobes have been developed by single-step green synthetic methods, offering characteristic absorption and dual emission properties, which allows to develop quick sensors for ROS sensing. Further, surface modified nanomaterials exhibited good biocompatibility and effective cellular internalization, thereby allowing to visualize ROS in cells with high sensitivity. The colorimetric sensing of ROS was based on the significant change in the absorption spectra of nanomaterials whereas fluorescence sensing was based on either fluorescence quenching or restoring of fluorescence with the introduction of ROS. The fluorescence detection of ROS was mainly due to FRET, PET, PCT and IFE between nanoprobes and other ligand systems in the presence of ROS. Importantly, surface modified nanomaterials-based optical sensors (colorimetry and fluorometry) have demonstrated their extraordinary selectivity towards specific ROS in the presence of other ROS and chemical species. Because of their outstanding optical properties, nanomaterial-based optical nanosensors for ROS sensing possess several features such as rapid detection, selectivity, and sensitivity, which endows them as real readers for on-site monitoring and imaging of ROS in environmental and biological samples.
